message.content:
I need to build endpoint-to-scope mappings for 5 APIs (2 social + 3 finance). For each, I need a JSON file mapping REST API endpoints to OAuth scopes. This is for a proxy-based policy enforcement system.

Output format:
```json
{
  "provider": "linkedin",
  "apiHost": "api.linkedin.com",
  "basePath": "/v2",
  "scopeMap": [
    {
      "method": "GET|POST|PATCH|DELETE",
      "pathPattern": "/v2/...",
      "sufficientScopes": ["r_liteprofile"],
      "description": "..."
    }
  ],
  "allScopes": [...]
}
```

Use `*` for variable path segments.

## 1. LinkedIn (api.linkedin.com/v2)
LinkedIn OAuth scopes: openid, profile, email, w_member_social, r_liteprofile, r_emailaddress, r_basicprofile, r_organization_social, w_organization_social, rw_organization_admin, r_ads, r_ads_reporting, rw_ads, r_1st_connections_size, r_organization_followers, w_member_social

Endpoints:
- Profile (get own profile via /v2/me, get profile by ID)
- Email (get primary email /v2/emailAddress)
- Connections
- Posts / shares (create, get, delete UGC posts)
- Organization pages (get, list, posts)
- Comments (list, create, delete)
- Reactions / likes (list, create, delete)
- Images / videos (upload, register)
- Analytics / follower stats
- Messages (if accessible)

Base path: /v2 (some newer endpoints use /rest)

## 2. Instagram (graph.instagram.com or graph.facebook.com)
Instagram uses the Facebook Graph API. Scopes: instagram_basic, instagram_content_publish, instagram_manage_comments, instagram_manage_insights, instagram_shopping_tag_products, pages_show_list, pages_read_engagement, business_management

Endpoints (via Graph API):
- User profile (GET /me, GET /{user-id})
- Media (GET /{user-id}/media, GET /{media-id}, POST /{user-id}/media for publishing)
- Media publishing (POST /{user-id}/media_publish)
- Comments (GET /{media-id}/comments, POST /{media-id}/comments, DELETE /{comment-id})
- Replies (GET /{comment-id}/replies, POST /{comment-id}/replies)
- Insights (GET /{user-id}/insights, GET /{media-id}/insights)
- Stories (GET /{user-id}/stories)
- Hashtag search (GET /ig_hashtag_search, GET /{hashtag-id}/top_media, GET /{hashtag-id}/recent_media)
- Content publishing (container creation + publish)
- Mentions (GET /{user-id}/tags)

Use api host: graph.instagram.com or graph.facebook.com

## 3. Stripe (api.stripe.com/v1)
Stripe uses API keys, not OAuth scopes traditionally. But for policy enforcement, define semantic scopes:
- charges.read, charges.write, customers.read, customers.write, invoices.read, invoices.write, subscriptions.read, subscriptions.write, products.read, products.write, prices.read, prices.write, payment_intents.read, payment_intents.write, payment_methods.read, payment_methods.write, refunds.read, refunds.write, balance.read, payouts.read, payouts.write, transfers.read, transfers.write, disputes.read, disputes.write, events.read, webhook_endpoints.read, webhook_endpoints.write, accounts.read, accounts.write, files.read, files.write, coupons.read, coupons.write, checkout_sessions.read, checkout_sessions.write, setup_intents.read, setup_intents.write

Endpoints:
- Charges (list, get, create, capture, update)
- Customers (list, get, create, update, delete)
- PaymentIntents (list, get, create, update, confirm, capture, cancel)
- PaymentMethods (list, get, create, attach, detach)
- SetupIntents (list, get, create, update, confirm, cancel)
- Invoices (list, get, create, update, delete, finalize, pay, send, void)
- Subscriptions (list, get, create, update, cancel, resume)
- Products (list, get, create, update, delete)
- Prices (list, get, create, update)
- Refunds (list, get, create, update)
- Balance (get)
- Balance transactions (list, get)
- Payouts (list, get, create, cancel)
- Transfers (list, get, create, update, reversals)
- Disputes (list, get, update, close)
- Events (list, get)
- Webhook endpoints (list, get, create, update, delete)
- Checkout sessions (list, get, create, expire)
- Coupons (list, get, create, update, delete)
- Files (list, get, create)
- Accounts (list, get, create, update, delete) - Connect
- Tax rates, credit notes, etc.

Base path: /v1

## 4. QuickBooks (quickbooks.api.intuit.com/v3)
QuickBooks Online scopes: com.intuit.quickbooks.accounting, com.intuit.quickbooks.payment, openid, profile, email, phone, address

For granular semantic scopes, define:
- accounting.read, accounting.write, payment.read, payment.write

Endpoints:
- Customers (query, get, create, update)
- Invoices (query, get, create, update, delete, send, void)
- Payments (query, get, create, update, delete, void)
- Bills (query, get, create, update, delete)
- Vendors (query, get, create, update)
- Items/Products (query, get, create, update)
- Accounts (query, get, create, update)
- Estimates (query, get, create, update, delete, send)
- PurchaseOrders (query, get, create, update, delete)
- Purchases (query, get, create, update, delete)
- JournalEntries (query, get, create, update, delete)
- TaxCodes, TaxRates
- Company info (get)
- Reports (profit & loss, balance sheet, etc.)
- Attachments (upload, download, list)

Base path: /v3/company/{companyId} — use /v3/company/*

## 5. Xero (api.xero.com/api.xro/2.0)
Xero OAuth scopes: openid, profile, email, accounting.transactions, accounting.transactions.read, accounting.reports.read, accounting.journals.read, accounting.settings, accounting.settings.read, accounting.contacts, accounting.contacts.read, accounting.attachments, accounting.attachments.read, payroll.employees, payroll.employees.read, payroll.timesheets, payroll.timesheets.read, payroll.payslip, payroll.payslip.read, files, files.read, assets, assets.read, projects, projects.read, bankfeeds

Endpoints:
- Contacts (list, get, create, update, archive)
- Invoices (list, get, create, update, void, email)
- Accounts (list, get, create, update, delete, archive)
- Bank transactions (list, get, create, update)
- Payments (list, get, create, delete)
- Credit notes (list, get, create, update)
- Items (list, get, create, update, delete)
- Manual journals (list, get, create, update)
- Organisations (get)
- Reports (balance sheet, P&L, trial balance, etc.)
- Tax rates (list, get, create, update)
- Currencies (list, get, create)
- Employees (list, get, create, update)
- Purchase orders (list, get, create, update)
- Quotes (list, get, create, update)
- Attachments (list, get, create)
- Branding themes (list, get)
- Tracking categories (list, get, create, update, delete)

Base path: /api.xro/2.0

Write:
- /workspace/scope-maps/linkedin.json
- /workspace/scope-maps/instagram.json
- /workspace/scope-maps/stripe.json
- /workspace/scope-maps/quickbooks.json
- /workspace/scope-maps/xero.json

Search the web for each provider's API reference docs. Be thorough!

Here's a summary of the search results for **QuickBooks Online API v3 endpoints, OAuth scopes, and documentation** for 2025–2026:

---

## OAuth 2.0 Scopes

The scope is a string value that is either `com.intuit.quickbooks.accounting` or `com.intuit.quickbooks.payment`. When integrating through unified platforms, the QuickBooks integration uses the same Accounting scope (`com.intuit.quickbooks.accounting`) alongside identity scopes (`openid`, `email`, `profile`).

QuickBooks Online uses OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow. You must handle OAuth correctly, manage token lifecycles, design for multi-tenant behavior, implement incremental sync, and respect rate limits per company.

## API v3 Endpoints

Key v3 endpoints include:
- **Read a customer:** `GET /v3/company/<realmID>/customer/<customerId>`
- **Create an invoice:** `POST /v3/company/<realmID>/invoice`
- **Query a bill:** `GET /v3/company/<realmID>/query?query=<selectStatement>`
- **Generate a purchase order:** `POST /v3/company/<realmID>/purchaseorder`
- **Query a balance sheet report:** `GET /v3/company/<realmID>/reports/BalanceSheet?<name>=<value>[&...]`

These vary slightly depending on whether you're using the sandbox or production environment.

QuickBooks Online API has a list of API endpoints that support creating new entities. For example, you can create a new Invoice, a new SalesReceipt, or a new Item through QuickBooks Online API.

## Rate Limits

Both environments allow you to make 500 requests per minute per realm ID. However, the sandbox environment has a limit of 40 emails/day per realm, while the production environment caps concurrent requests to 10 per realm ID and app.

## 2025 Changes

While the QuickBooks API was historically free with no volume restrictions, Intuit introduced a significant change in 2025 with the launch of the Intuit App Partner Program.

The Builder Tier's 500,000 CorePlus API calls per month limit catches integrations off-guard. Once exceeded, services experience failed syncs and complete interruptions until the next billing cycle.

In 2025, OAuth modifications led to simultaneous failures across Power BI and numerous third-party integrations. Reddit users report that even minor OAuth updates from Intuit trigger platform-wide integration failures until patches are deployed.

## Official Documentation Links

- **Authentication & Authorization:** [developer.intuit.com – Authentication and Authorization](https://developer.intuit.com/app/developer/qbo/docs/develop/authentication-and-authorization)
- **OAuth 2.0 Setup:** [developer.intuit.com – OAuth 2.0](https://developer.intuit.com/app/developer/qbo/docs/develop/authentication-and-authorization/oauth-2.0)
- **OAuth 2.0 FAQ:** [developer.intuit.com – OAuth FAQ](https://developer.intuit.com/app/developer/qbo/docs/develop/authentication-and-authorization/faq)

These official Intuit pages are the authoritative source for the latest OAuth 2.0 and API endpoint documentation.

Here's what I found about the Stripe API v1 REST endpoints and documentation:

## Stripe API v1 REST Endpoints — Overview

The Stripe API is organized around REST. It has predictable resource-oriented URLs, accepts form-encoded request bodies, returns JSON-encoded responses, and uses standard HTTP response codes, authentication, and verbs.

### API Namespaces (v1 vs v2)

API v1: The `/v1` namespace includes most of the existing Stripe API today. API v2: The `/v2` namespace includes endpoints that use `/v2` design patterns. All server-side SDKs support APIs in the `/v2` namespace.

### Current API Version

The latest Stripe API version appears to be **2026-02-25.clover**, as seen in their current reference pages (e.g., the Webhook Endpoints reference shows version `2026-02-25.clover`).

### Example v1 Resource Endpoints

Here are some example v1 endpoint groups found in the documentation:

- **Accounts**: `POST /v1/accounts`, `POST /v1/accounts/:id`, `GET /v1/accounts/:id`, `GET /v1/accounts`, `DELETE /v1/accounts/:id`, `POST /v1/accounts/:id/reject`

- **Webhook Endpoints**: `POST /v1/webhook_endpoints`, `POST /v1/webhook_endpoints/:id`, `GET /v1/webhook_endpoints/:id`, `GET /v1/webhook_endpoints`, `DELETE /v1/webhook_endpoints/:id`

### OpenAPI Specification (Complete List of All Endpoints)

For a **complete list** of all v1 (and v2) endpoints, Stripe publishes OpenAPI specs:

New versions of Stripe's OpenAPI specifications have been added to the `stripe/openapi` repository, covering all endpoints and events in the v1 and v2 API namespaces in a single file. The new files are available in two directories: `/latest/` for the latest generally available (GA) release. OpenAPI files in `/openapi/`, which contain only v1 endpoints and events, will continue to be updated as usual with each new API version.

Developers of third-party SDKs and custom Stripe clients that are powered by the OpenAPI specifications in `stripe/openapi` can use the new unified files to power their code generation.

### API Versioning

Your API version controls the API and webhook behavior you see (for example, what parameters you can include in requests, what properties you see in responses, and so on). Your version gets set the first time you make an API request. Each major release, such as Acacia, includes changes that aren't backward-compatible with previous releases. Upgrading to a new major release can require updates to existing code. Each monthly release includes only backward-compatible changes and uses the same name as the last major release. You can safely upgrade to a new monthly release without breaking any existing code.

### Key Resources

- **Full API Reference**: [https://docs.stripe.com/api](https://docs.stripe.com/api)
- **API Upgrades / Changelog**: [https://docs.stripe.com/upgrades](https://docs.stripe.com/upgrades)
- **OpenAPI Specs (GitHub)**: [https://github.com/stripe/openapi](https://github.com/stripe/openapi) — This is the best resource for a **machine-readable complete list** of all v1 REST endpoints.
- **API v2 Overview**: [https://docs.stripe.com/api-v2-overview](https://docs.stripe.com/api-v2-overview)

The full API reference at `docs.stripe.com/api` contains the complete categorized list of all v1 endpoints across dozens of resource types (Charges, Customers, PaymentIntents, Subscriptions, Invoices, Products, Prices, Refunds, Disputes, Balance, Payouts, and many more). For a programmatic/complete enumeration, the OpenAPI spec on GitHub is the definitive source.

Here's a summary of what's happening with Xero API endpoints, OAuth 2.0 scopes, and related documentation for 2025–2026:

## Xero API OAuth 2.0 Scopes — Key Updates

### Major Change: Granular Scopes Replacing Broad Scopes

Xero is updating the scopes for its Accounting API to give developers more control and security. Broad scopes are being replaced by granular scopes.

- Apps created on or after **2 March 2026** will use the new granular scopes. Instead of broad access, apps will now request only the exact permissions needed, giving users more control during the OAuth 2.0 flow and making it clear how their data is used.

### Transition Timeline

- Apps created before 2 March 2026 will have until **September 2027** to move from broad scopes to granular scopes, though updating as soon as possible is recommended.
- Granular scopes will be available to request from **April 2026**. If you're building a new app after 2 March 2026, your app will only have access to the new granular scopes.
- Once you start using granular scopes, your app will be able to use a mix of broad and granular scopes until all customers migrate to granular scopes by September 2027.

### Example of Granular Scope Changes

The broad `accounting.transactions` scope is now split into more specific scopes such as: `accounting.invoices` (credit notes, invoices, linked transactions, purchase orders, quotes, repeating invoices, and items), `accounting.payments` (batch payments, overpayments, payments, and prepayments), and others.

### Available Scope Categories

Xero's scopes documentation covers: offline access, user scopes, OpenID Connect, organisation scopes, Accounting API, Payroll API (Australia, UK, New Zealand), Files API, Assets API, Projects API, Payment services, Bank feeds, Finance API, Practice Manager API, eInvoicing API, and non-tenanted scopes.

### OAuth 2.0 Auth Flow

The standard authorization code flow involves: sending a user to authorize your app (with scopes and state), users being redirected back with a code, exchanging the code, receiving tokens (with expiry), checking authorized tenants, calling the API, refreshing tokens, and revoking tokens.

### Pricing & Terms Changes (2025–2026)

- Beginning on 2 March 2026, Xero is moving to a commercial tiering model based on connections and API usage, no longer pricing based on a revenue-share model. Importantly, API data may not be used to train AI/ML models.
- Xero is transitioning to five tiers: Starter, Core, Plus, Advanced, and Enterprise, based on connections and data consumption. These changes take effect on 2 March 2026.

### Key Resources

- **Scopes documentation**: [developer.xero.com/documentation/guides/oauth2/scopes/](https://developer.xero.com/documentation/guides/oauth2/scopes/)
- **OAuth 2.0 overview**: [developer.xero.com/documentation/guides/oauth2/overview/](https://developer.xero.com/documentation/guides/oauth2/overview/)

Here's a summary of the latest information on the **LinkedIn REST API v2 endpoints, OAuth scopes, and documentation** for 2025–2026:

---

## OAuth 2.0 Authentication

LinkedIn's API uses **OAuth 2.0** (Open Authorization) for user authorization and API authentication. OAuth 2.0 allows websites or applications to access user data securely, ensuring that client applications can access specific resources on behalf of a user without sharing the user's credentials.

### Authorization Flows

LinkedIn uses OAuth 2.0 to ensure that all API usage is secure and permission-based. There are two main authorization flows:
- **3-legged OAuth (Member Authorization):** Requires a user to give permission for your app to access their data. LinkedIn now uses **OpenID Connect** for member sign-in.
- **2-legged OAuth (Application Authorization):** Used to access non-user-specific data, such as analytics or company page stats.

### Authorization Endpoint

The authorization endpoint is `GET https://www.linkedin.com/oauth/v2/authorization`. The scopes available to your app depend on which Products or Partner Programs your app has access to, and this information is available in the LinkedIn Developer Portal.

### Access Token Endpoint

The access token endpoint is `POST https://www.linkedin.com/oauth/v2/accessToken`, using a `Content-Type` header of `x-www-form-urlencoded`.

---

## Key OAuth Scopes

Your app needs the required scopes such as `r_emailaddress`, `w_member_social`, `r_liteprofile`, `w_organization_social`, and others based on your chosen products.

Additional scopes used in practice include:

- `w_member_social` (default for posting)
- `w_organization_social` (special request)
- `r_organization_social` (special request)
- `r_organization_admin` (special request)

---

## 2025–2026 API Updates

**Community Management API:** This API now manages content creation including text posts, images, videos (with thumbnails and captions), carousel posts, polls, and comments/reactions on behalf of organizations and brands.

**Video Analytics Endpoint:** Community Management API partners can retrieve VIDEO_PLAY, VIDEO_WATCH_TIME, and VIDEO_VIEWER metrics for member video posts via the new `GET /memberCreatorVideoAnalytics` endpoint (starting with version 202506).

LinkedIn deprecated the classic versioning format and each version is now supported for at least one year. To use the API, you must include the version header in every request. Additionally, the `isDsc` parameter in the Posts API is deprecated; organic and sponsored posts are returned together by default.

---

## Token Introspection

LinkedIn introduced a **Token Inspector Tool** and Token Introspection API to get a list of scopes granted to an access token via the Authorization Code Flow (3-legged OAuth).

The introspection response includes details such as: scope (comma-separated list of permission scopes), client_id, created_at, expires_at, authorized_at, and auth_type (which can be "2L" for application tokens, "3L" for member tokens, or "Enterprise_User" for enterprise tokens).

---

## Common Troubleshooting

Common issues when working with LinkedIn OAuth scopes include: incorrect application permissions in the LinkedIn Developer Portal, scopes not properly requested in the OAuth authorization URL, expiration or revocation of previously granted tokens, and API versioning issues that affect scope availability.

---

### Key Resources
- **Official OAuth Docs:** [Microsoft Learn – LinkedIn Authorization Code Flow](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/linkedin/shared/authentication/authorization-code-flow)
- **OAuth 2.0 Overview:** [Microsoft Learn – LinkedIn Authentication](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/linkedin/shared/authentication/authentication)
- **LinkedIn Developer Portal:** [LinkedIn Developers](https://www.linkedin.com/developers/)
- **2026 API Guide:** [OutX – LinkedIn API Guide](https://www.outx.ai/blog/linkedin-api-guide)

Links: [{"title":"Instagram Graph API: Complete Developer Guide for 2026","url":"https://elfsight.com/blog/instagram-graph-api-complete-developer-guide-for-2026/"},{"title":"Instagram API Documentation 2026: Complete Developer Guide","url":"https://getlate.dev/blog/instagram-api"},{"title":"Oauth Authorize - Instagram Platform - Documentation","url":"https://developers.facebook.com/docs/instagram-platform/reference/oauth-authorize/"},{"title":"Instagram API Documentation Guide 2025","url":"https://gwaa.net/api-endpoint-documentation-instagram-developer-resources"},{"title":"After Basic Display EOL: How Instagram’s 2026 API Rules Reshape Scheduling, Embeds, and Creator Tools","url":"https://storrito.com/resources/Instagram-API-2026/"},{"title":"A comprehensive guide for implementing Instagram content publishing using the Instagram Platform API (Instagram Direct Login) that launched in July 2024. · GitHub","url":"https://gist.github.com/PrenSJ2/0213e60e834e66b7e09f7f93999163fc"},{"title":"How to Begin Using the Instagram and Facebook Graph APIs for Mobile Apps","url":"https://medium.com/@python-javascript-php-html-css/how-to-begin-using-the-instagram-and-facebook-graph-apis-for-mobile-apps-c810bbfd0af3"},{"title":"GitHub - Z786ZA/instagram-api-documentation: instagram api documentation reference guide · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/Z786ZA/instagram-api-documentation"},{"title":"Instagram Graph API Guide 2026: Authentication, Limits & ...","url":"https://getlate.dev/blog/instagram-graph-api"},{"title":"A Complete Guide to Instagram API Documentation","url":"https://projectmanagers.net/a-complete-guide-to-instagram-api-documentation/"}]

Here's a comprehensive overview of the **Instagram Graph API endpoints, OAuth permissions, and scopes** based on the latest documentation for 2025–2026:

---

## Current API Landscape (2025–2026)

On December 4, 2024, the Instagram Basic Display API reached end-of-life. This API previously allowed read-only access to personal Instagram accounts through a simple OAuth flow. After this date, it no longer functions.

In 2026, all integrations must use the Instagram Graph and Messaging APIs for business and creator accounts, reshaping common use cases for scheduling, embeds, and analytics workflows.

Personal Instagram accounts are no longer supported via third-party APIs. Only Business and Creator accounts can connect to applications.

---

## OAuth 2.0 Authentication

Instagram uses OAuth 2.0 for authentication, ensuring secure access to users' data. This process involves generating an access token, which you will need to include in your API requests. The permissions system controls the level of access you have, ranging from reading profile data to posting content on behalf of a user.

### Two Authentication Approaches

**1. Business Login (Instagram Direct Login)**

The Business Login approach uses OAuth 2.0 to authenticate users directly through Instagram. This method generates Instagram User access tokens that represent a specific Instagram Business or Creator account's permissions.

Use cases include building apps where users authenticate directly through Instagram, accessing data from a single specific Instagram account, and scenarios where you want minimal Facebook infrastructure involvement.

**2. Facebook Login**

The Facebook Login approach uses Instagram accounts that are connected to Facebook Pages. This method is more common for enterprise applications and integrations with Facebook's ecosystem.

---

## OAuth Flow

The authorization flow follows these steps:

1. **Redirect to authorization**: `GET /oauth/authorize` with your `client_id`, `redirect_uri`, `response_type=code`, and `scope` parameters.
2. **Exchange code for token**: After user authorization, exchange the authorization code for a short-lived access token via `POST /oauth/access_token`.
3. **Long-lived token**: Exchange the short-lived token for a long-lived token (60 days) via `GET /access_token` with `grant_type=ig_exchange_token`.

---

## Permission Scopes

### Business Login Scopes:
Key scopes include:
- `instagram_basic` — Read basic Instagram profile and media data
- `instagram_graph_user_profile` — Access to user profile information
- `instagram_manage_messages` — If managing direct messages

For content publishing, use `instagram_business_basic,instagram_business_content_publish`.

### Facebook Login Permissions:
Required permissions include `pages_show_list` — Access your list of Facebook Pages.

---

## Key Endpoints

Instagram offers a variety of endpoints, each serving specific purposes: access user profiles (username, profile picture, bio), retrieve media such as photos, videos, and stories, and interact with posts, allowing actions like commenting, liking, or scheduling content.

### Hashtag Search Endpoints:
Hashtag endpoints include `GET /ig_hashtag_search`, `GET /{ig-hashtag-id}/top_media`, `GET /{ig-hashtag-id}/recent_media`, and `GET /{ig-user-id}/recently_searched_hashtags`. Rate limiting note: You can search up to 30 unique hashtags per week per Instagram account. After 7 days, the limit resets for previously searched hashtags.

### Comments Endpoint:
The ability to read, reply to, and delete comments is handled through the media-specific comments endpoint: `/{media-id}/comments`.

---

## Important Deprecations (2025)

As of January 8, 2025, Meta deprecated several Instagram Insights API metrics starting with Graph API v21. Deprecated fields include `video_views` (for non-Reels content), `email_contacts` (time series), `profile_views`, `website_clicks`, `phone_call_clicks`, and `text_message_clicks`. Update your implementations to avoid breaking changes.

---

## Rate Limits

Instagram enforces rate limits to prevent abuse. Typically, each app is allowed around 200 API calls per user per hour, depending on endpoint type and account tier.

For direct messaging automation, Meta exposes the Instagram Messaging API with strict limits (e.g., 200 automated DMs per hour per account in 2026).

---

## Key Considerations

The Graph API's authentication and permission model requires app review for most production integrations, and tokens must be long-lived and refreshed per Meta's OAuth flows.

Requesting unnecessary permissions (asking for scopes your app doesn't really need) is a massive red flag for Meta App reviewers and a common cause of rejection.

Official APIs in 2026 only allow access to accounts authorized by the user (business/creator). Public discovery endpoints for arbitrary users or hashtag searches are not available.

For full official documentation, visit [Meta's Instagram Platform docs](https://developers.facebook.com/docs/instagram-platform/reference/oauth-authorize/).

# LinkedIn 3-Legged OAuth Flow - LinkedIn | Microsoft Learn

The Authorization Code Flow is used for applications to request permission from a LinkedIn member to access their account data. The level of access or profile detail is explicitly requested using the `scope` parameter during the authorization process outlined below. This workflow will send a consent prompt to a selected member, and once approved your application may begin making API calls on behalf of that member.

This approval process ensures that LinkedIn members are aware of what level of detail an application may access or action it may perform on their behalf.

If multiple scopes are requested, the user must consent to all of them and may not select the individual scopes. For the benefit of your LinkedIn users, please ensure that your application requests the least number of scope permissions.

Note

**Generate a Token Manually Using the LinkedIn Developer Portal** The LinkedIn Developer Portal has a token generator for manually creating tokens. Visit the [LinkedIn Developer Portal Token Generator](https://www.linkedin.com/developers/tools/oauth/token-generator) or follow the steps outlined in [LinkedIn Developer Portal Tools.](developer-portal-tools)

## Authorization Code Flow

1. Configure your application in the Developer Portal to obtain *Client ID* and *Client Secret*.
2. Your application directs the browser to LinkedIn's OAuth 2.0 authorization page where the member authenticates.
3. After authentication, LinkedIn's authorization server passes an authorization code to your application.
4. Your application sends this code to LinkedIn and LinkedIn returns an access token.
5. Your application uses this token to make API calls on behalf of the member.

![auth flow](../../media/oauth-3-legged-flow_highres.png)

## How to Implement 3-legged OAuth

Follow the steps given below to implement the 3-legged OAuth for LinkedIn APIs:

### Prerequisites

- A LinkedIn Developer application to [create a new application](https://www.linkedin.com/developer/apps/new?csrfToken=ajax%3A8674117952230020505) or [select your existing application](https://www.linkedin.com/developers/apps)
- Prior authorization access granted for at least one 3-legged OAuth permission.

The permission request workflow is outlined in the [Getting Access](getting-access) section.

## Step 1: Configure Your Application

1. Select your application in the [LinkedIn Developer Portal](https://www.linkedin.com/developers/apps).
2. Click the **Auth** tab to view your application credentials.
3. Add the redirect (callback) URL via `HTTPS` to your server.

Note

LinkedIn servers will only communicate with URLs that you have identified as trusted.

- URLs must be absolute:
    - `https://dev.example.com/auth/linkedin/callback`
    - *not*`/auth/linkedin/callback`
- Parameters are ignored:
    - `https://dev.example.com/auth/linkedin/callback?id=1`
    - *will be*`https://dev.example.com/auth/linkedin/callback`
- URLs cannot include a #
    - `https://dev.example.com/auth/linkedin/callback#linkedin` is invalid.

If you are using Postman to test this flow, use `https://oauth.pstmn.io/v1/callback` as your redirect URL and enable **Authorize using browser**.

![Redirect URLs](../../media/oauth_redirect_urls.png)

Each application is assigned a unique **Client ID** (Consumer key/API key) and **Client Secret**. Please make a note of these values as they will be integrated into your application. Your **Client Secret** protects your application's security so be sure to keep it secure!

![OAuth Values](../../media/oauth_values.png)

Warning

Do not share your *Client Secret* value with anyone, and **do not** pass it in the URL when making API calls, or URI query-string parameters, or post in support forums, chat, etc.

## Step 2: Request an Authorization Code

To request an authorization code, you must direct the member's browser to LinkedIn's OAuth 2.0 authorization page, where the member either accepts or denies your application's permission request. The user may first be asked to log in to LinkedIn, either by entering their LinkedIn credentials or by using one of LinkedIn’s supported login methods (such as "Sign in with Google", "Sign in with Apple", or passkey). All authentication is performed securely on LinkedIn's domain.

Important

Social login (Google/Apple) and passkey login are currently supported on desktop and Android web browsers only. To enable these login options on other platforms (such as native apps), include the query parameter `enable_extended_login=true` while calling `/oauth/v2/authorization`. To ensure the login options work seamlessly, please follow the [standard OAuth specification](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8252) when invoking OAuth. If you want to opt out from the new login option submit a [Zendesk](https://linkedin.zendesk.com/hc/en-us) request to the LinkedIn support team.

Once the request is made, one of the following occurs:

1. If it is a first-time request, the permission request timed out, or was manually revoked by the member: the browser is redirected to LinkedIn's authorization consent window.
2. If there is an existing permission grant from the member: the authorization screen is bypassed and the member is immediately redirected to the URL provided in the `redirect_uri` query parameter.

![auth li](../../media/authorize-linkedin.png)

When the member completes the authorization process, the browser is redirected to the URL provided in the `redirect_uri` query parameter.

Note

If the `scope` permissions are changed in your app, your users must re-authenticate to ensure that they have explicitly granted your application all of the permissions that it is requesting on their behalf.

```https
GET https://www.linkedin.com/oauth/v2/authorization
```

| Parameter | Type | Description | Required |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| response\_type | string | The value of this field should always be: `code` | Yes |
| client\_id | string | The *API Key* value generated when you registered your application. | Yes |
| redirect\_uri | url | The URI your users are sent back to after authorization. This value must match one of the *Redirect URLs* defined in your [application configuration](https://www.linkedin.com/developers/). For example, `https://dev.example.com/auth/linkedin/callback`. | Yes |
| state | string | A unique string value of your choice that is hard to guess. Used to prevent [CSRF](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross-site_request_forgery). For example, `state=DCEeFWf45A53sdfKef424`. | No |
| scope | string | URL-encoded, space-delimited list of member permissions your application is requesting on behalf of the user. These must be explicitly requested. For example, `scope=liteprofile%20emailaddress%20w_member_social`. See [Permissions](authentication) and [Best Practices for Application Development](../api-guide/best-practices/application-development?context=linkedin/context) for additional information. | Yes |

The scopes available to your app depend on which Products or Partner Programs your app has access to. This information is available in the [LinkedIn Developer Portal](https://www.linkedin.com/developers/apps). Your app's Auth tab will show current scopes available. You can apply for new Products under the Products tab. If approved, your app will have access to new scopes.

#### Sample Request

```https
GET https://www.linkedin.com/oauth/v2/authorization?response_type=code&client_id={your_client_id}&redirect_uri={your_callback_url}&state=foobar&scope=liteprofile%20emailaddress%20w_member_social
```

Once redirected, the member is presented with LinkedIn's authentication screen. This identifies your application and outlines the particular member permissions/scopes that your application is requesting. You can change the logo and application name in the LinkedIn Developer Portal under My apps > [Settings](https://www.linkedin.com/developers/apps).

![auth li](../../media/authorize-linkedin.png)

#### Member Approves Request

By providing valid LinkedIn credentials and clicking **Allow**, the member approves your application's request to access their member data and interact with LinkedIn on their behalf. This approval instructs LinkedIn to redirect the member to the redirect URL that you defined in your `redirect_uri` parameter.

```https
https://dev.example.com/auth/linkedin/callback?state=foobar&code=AQTQmah11lalyH65DAIivsjsAQV5P-1VTVVebnLl_SCiyMXoIjDmJ4s6rO1VBGP5Hx2542KaR_eNawkrWiCiAGxIaV-TCK-mkxDISDak08tdaBzgUYfnTJL1fHRoDWCcC2L6LXBCR_z2XHzeWSuqTkR1_jO8CeV9E_WshsJBgE-PWElyvsmfuEXLQbCLfj8CHasuLafFpGb0glO4d7M
```

Attached to the `redirect_uri` are two important URL arguments that you need to read from the request:

- `code` — The OAuth 2.0 authorization code.
- `state` — A value used to test for possible [CSRF](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross-site_request_forgery) attacks.

The `code` is a value that you exchange with LinkedIn for an OAuth 2.0 access token in the next step of the authentication process. For security reasons, the authorization code has a 30-minute lifespan and must be used immediately. If it expires, you must repeat all of the previous steps to request another authorization code.

Warning

Before you use the authorization code, your application should ensure that the value returned in the `state` parameter matches the `state` value from your original authorization code request. This ensures that you are dealing with the real member and not a malicious script. If the state values do not match, you are likely the victim of a [CSRF](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross-site_request_forgery) attack and your application should return a `401 Unauthorized` error code in response.

#### Failed Requests

If the member chooses to cancel, or the request fails for any reason, the client is redirected to your `redirect_uri` with the following additional query parameters appended:

- `error`- A code indicating one of these errors:
    - `user_cancelled_login` - The member declined to log in to their LinkedIn account.
    - `user_cancelled_authorize` - The member refused to authorize the permissions request from your application.
- `error_description` - A URL-encoded textual description that summarizes the error.
- `state` - A value passed by your application to prevent [CSRF](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross-site_request_forgery) attacks.

For more error details, see here

## Step 3: Exchange Authorization Code for an Access Token

The next step is to get an access token for your application using the authorization code from the previous step.

```https
POST https://www.linkedin.com/oauth/v2/accessToken
```

To do this, make the following HTTP POST request with a `Content-Type` header of `x-www-form-urlencoded` using the following parameters:

| Parameter | Type | Description | Required |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| grant\_type | string | The value of this field should always be: `authorization_code` | Yes |
| code | string | The authorization code you received in Step 2. | Yes |
| client\_id | string | The Client ID value generated in Step 1. | Yes |
| client\_secret | string | The Secret Key value generated in Step 1. See the [Best Practices Guide](../api-guide/best-practices/secure-applications?context=linkedin/context) for ways to keep your `client_secret` value secure. | Yes |
| redirect\_uri | url | The same `redirect_uri` value that you passed in the previous step. | Yes |

#### Sample Request

**https**

```https
POST  https://www.linkedin.com/oauth/v2/accessToken
 
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
grant_type=authorization_code
code={authorization_code_from_step2_response}
client_id={your_client_id}
client_secret={your_client_secret}
redirect_uri={your_callback_url}
```

**curl**

```curl
curl --location --request POST 'https://www.linkedin.com/oauth/v2/accessToken' \
--header 'Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \
--data-urlencode 'grant_type=authorization_code' \
--data-urlencode 'code={authorization_code_from_step2_response}' \
--data-urlencode 'client_id={your_client_id}' \
--data-urlencode 'client_secret={your_client_secret}' \
--data-urlencode 'redirect_uri={your_callback_url}'
```

#### Response

A successful access token request returns a [JSON](http://www.json.org/) object containing the following fields:

| Parameter | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| access\_token | string | The access token for the application. This value must be kept secure as specified in the [API Terms of Use](https://www.linkedin.com/legal/l/api-terms-of-use). The length of access tokens is ~500 characters. We recommend that you plan for your application to handle tokens with length of at least 1000 characters to accommodate any future expansion plans. This applies to both access tokens and refresh tokens. |
| expires\_in | int | The number of seconds remaining until the token expires. Currently, all access tokens are issued with a 60-day lifespan. |
| refresh\_token | string | Your refresh token for the application. This token must be kept secure. |
| refresh\_token\_expires\_in | int | The number of seconds remaining until the refresh token expires. Refresh tokens usually have a longer lifespan than access tokens. |
| scope | string | URL-encoded, space-delimited list of member permissions your application has requested on behalf of the user. |

```json
{  
"access_token":"AQUvlL_DYEzvT2wz1QJiEPeLioeA",
"expires_in":5184000,
"scope":"r_basicprofile"
}
```

For more error details, refer to the API Error Details table.

Note

**Access Token Scopes and Lifetime** Access tokens stay valid until the number of seconds indicated in the `expires_in` field in the API response. You can go through the OAuth flow on multiple clients (browsers or devices) and simultaneously hold multiple valid access tokens if the same scope is requested. If you request a different scope than the previously granted scope, all the previous access tokens are invalidated.

## Step 4: Make Authenticated Requests

Once you've obtained an access token, you can start making authenticated API requests on behalf of the member by including an `Authorization` header in the HTTP call to LinkedIn's API.

#### Sample Request

```bash
curl -X GET 'https://api.linkedin.com/v2/me' \
-H 'Authorization: Bearer {INSERT_TOKEN}'
```

## Step 5: Refresh Access Token

Tip

To protect members' data, LinkedIn does not generate long-lived access tokens.

> 
> Make sure your application refreshes access tokens before they expire, to avoid unnecessarily sending your application's users through the authorization process again.

Refreshing an access token is a seamless user experience. To refresh an access token, go through the authorization process again to fetch a new token. This time however, in the refresh workflow, the authorization screen is bypassed, and the member is redirected to your redirect URL, provided the following conditions are met:

- The member is still logged into https://www.linkedin.com
- The member's current access token has not expired

If the member is no longer logged in to https://www.linkedin.com or their access token has expired, they are sent through the normal authorization process.

Programmatic refresh tokens are available for a limited set of partners. If this feature has been enabled for your application, see [Programmatic Refresh Tokens](programmatic-refresh-tokens?context=linkedin/context) for instructions.

## API Error Details

Following are the API errors and thier resolution for 3-legged OAuth. If you wish to view the standard HTTP status codes and thier meaning, see [Error Handling](../api-guide/concepts/error-handling) page.

#### /oauth/v2/authorization

| HTTP STATUS CODE | ERROR MESSAGE | ERROR DESCRIPTION | RESOLUTION |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 401 | Redirect\_uri doesn’t match | Redirect URI passed in the request does not match the redirect URI added to the developer application. | Ensure that the redirect URI passed in the request match the redirect URI added in the developer application under the Authorization tab. |
| 401 | Client\_id doesn’t match | Client ID passed in the request does not match the client ID of the developer application. | Ensure that the client ID passed is in match with the developer application. |
| 401 | Invalid scope | Permissions passed in the request is invalid | Ensure that the permissions sent in scope parameter is assigned to the developer application in the LinkedIn developer portal. |

#### /oauth/v2/accessToken

| HTTP STATUS CODE | ERROR MESSAGE | ERROR DESCRIPTION | RESOLUTION |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 401 | invalid\_request "Unable to retrieve access token: authorization code not found" | Authorization code sent is invalid or not found. | Check whether the sent authorization code is valid. |
| 400 | invalid\_request "A required parameter "redirect\_uri" is missing" | Redirect\_uri in the request is missing. It is a mandatory parameter. | Pass the redirect\_uri in the request to route user back to correct landing page. |
| 400 | invalid\_request "A required parameter "code" is missing" | Authorization code in the request is missing. It is a mandatory parameter. | Pass the Authorization code received as part of authorization API call. |
| 400 | invalid\_request "A required parameter "grant\_type" is missing" | Grant type in the request is missing. It is a mandatory parameter. | Add grant\_type as "authorization\_code" in the request. |
| 400 | invalid\_request "A required parameter "client\_id" is missing" | Client ID in the request is missing. It is a mandatory parameter. | Pass the client id of the app in request. |
| 400 | invalid\_request "A required parameter "client\_secret" is missing" | Client Secret in the request is missing. It is a mandatory parameter. | Pass the client secret of the app in request. |
| 400 | invalid\_redirect\_uri "Unable to retrieve access token: appid/redirect uri/code verifier does not match authorization code. Or authorization code expired. Or external member binding exists" | Invalid redirect uri is passed in the request. | Pass the right redirect uri tagged to the developer application. |
| 400 | invalid\_redirect\_uri "Unable to retrieve access token: appid/redirect uri/code verifier does not match authorization code. Or authorization code expired. Or external member binding exists" | Invalid redirect uri is passed in the request. | Pass the right redirect uri tagged to the developer application. |
| 400 | invalid\_redirect\_uri "Unable to retrieve access token: appid/redirect uri/code verifier does not match authorization code. Or authorization code expired. Or external member binding exists" | Invalid Authorization code is sent as part of the request. | Authorization code expired; re-authenticate member to generate new authorization code and pass the fresh authorization code to exchange for access token. |

Here's a comprehensive overview of the Xero Accounting API REST endpoints, scopes, and key resources:

## Xero Accounting API — REST Endpoints & Scopes

### Key REST Endpoints

The Xero component allows you to manage your invoices, payments, items, and contacts through the Xero REST API. With over 100 endpoints available, developers can access everything from bank transactions to inventory management through standardised HTTP requests.

#### **Contacts** (`/api.xro/2.0/Contacts`)
The Contacts endpoint supports GET, POST, and PUT operations, with optional parameters including high volume threshold limits, paging (recommended), optimised use of the `where` filter, optimised filtering on a list of values, and a smaller lightweight response using the `summaryOnly` parameter.

#### **Invoices** (`/api.xro/2.0/Invoices`)
The Invoices endpoint supports GET, POST, and PUT operations, along with features like paging (recommended), range operators in where clauses, the `summaryOnly` parameter, retrieving the online invoice URL, creating PAID invoices, deleting/voiding invoices, uploading attachments, emailing invoices, and webhooks.

#### **Accounts** (`/api.xro/2.0/Accounts`)
The Accounts endpoint supports GET, PUT, POST, and DELETE operations, including uploading attachments and archiving accounts.

#### **Payments** (`/api.xro/2.0/Payments`)
The Payments endpoint supports multicurrency payments, GET with optional parameters, high volume threshold limits, paging (recommended), optimised filtering using the `where` parameter, PUT for creating single/multiple payments or reconciled payments, and POST for deleting payments.

### OAuth 2.0 Scopes

Primary scopes include `accounting.transactions` for invoices and bills, `accounting.contacts` for customer data, and `offline_access` for refresh tokens.

When setting up your integration, you should enter the scopes relevant to your integration. Always include the `offline_access` scope in order for authentication tokens to refresh automatically.

A typical scope configuration looks like:
`['openid', 'profile', 'email', 'accounting.transactions', 'offline_access']`

### API Limits & Best Practices

- Xero enforces a limit of 5,000 API calls per day per organisation. This limit resets at midnight UTC.
- You can create up to 50 invoices in a single POST request, with a maximum request size of 3.5MB. This bulk operation counts as one API call regardless of the number of invoices.
- Access tokens expire after 30 minutes. Implement automatic token refresh using the refresh token, which remains valid until explicitly revoked by the user.

### Authentication

OAuth 2.0 authentication is mandatory for all Xero API access. The process involves several steps that must be implemented correctly for secure access.

### Types & Codes Reference

Xero provides a comprehensive types and codes reference covering Account Class Types, Account Types, Account Status Codes, Bank Account Types, Contact Status Codes, Invoice Types, Invoice Status Codes, Payment Status Codes, Payment Terms, Payment Types, and many more.

### Useful Links
- **API Overview**: https://developer.xero.com/documentation/api/accounting/overview
- **Invoices**: https://developer.xero.com/documentation/api/accounting/invoices
- **Contacts**: https://developer.xero.com/documentation/api/accounting/contacts
- **Accounts**: https://developer.xero.com/documentation/api/accounting/accounts
- **Payments**: https://developer.xero.com/documentation/api/accounting/payments
- **Types & Codes**: https://developer.xero.com/documentation/api/accounting/types

Here's a comprehensive overview of the key **Stripe API v1** endpoints for the resources you asked about:

---

## 1. Charges (`/v1/charges`)

The direct Charges creation method is no longer recommended—the Payment Intents API should be used to initiate new payments instead. Confirmation of the PaymentIntent creates the Charge object used to request payment.

**Endpoints:**
- `POST /v1/charges` — Create a charge
- `GET /v1/charges/:id` — Retrieve a charge
- `POST /v1/charges/:id` — Update a charge
- `GET /v1/charges` — List all charges
- `POST /v1/charges/:id/capture` — Capture a charge
- `GET /v1/charges/search` — Search charges

You can search for charges you've previously created using Stripe's Search Query Language. Don't use search in read-after-write flows where strict consistency is necessary.

---

## 2. Customers (`/v1/customers`)

You can retrieve a Customer object with no additional parameters. It returns the Customer object for a valid identifier.

**Endpoints:**
- `POST /v1/customers` — Create a customer
- `GET /v1/customers/:id` — Retrieve a customer
- `POST /v1/customers/:id` — Update a customer
- `DELETE /v1/customers/:id` — Delete a customer
- `GET /v1/customers` — List all customers
- `GET /v1/customers/search` — Search customers

---

## 3. PaymentIntents (`/v1/payment_intents`)

A PaymentIntent guides you through the process of collecting a payment from your customer. Stripe recommends that you create exactly one PaymentIntent for each order or customer session in your system. You can reference the PaymentIntent later to see the history of payment attempts for a particular session. A PaymentIntent transitions through multiple statuses throughout its lifetime and ultimately creates at most one successful charge.

**Endpoints:**
- `POST /v1/payment_intents` — Create a PaymentIntent
- `GET /v1/payment_intents/:id` — Retrieve a PaymentIntent
- `POST /v1/payment_intents/:id` — Update a PaymentIntent
- `GET /v1/payment_intents` — List all PaymentIntents
- `POST /v1/payment_intents/:id/confirm` — Confirm a PaymentIntent
- `POST /v1/payment_intents/:id/capture` — Capture a PaymentIntent
- `POST /v1/payment_intents/:id/cancel` — Cancel a PaymentIntent
- `POST /v1/payment_intents/:id/increment_authorization` — Increment authorization
- `GET /v1/payment_intents/:id/amount_details_line_items` — List line items
- `GET /v1/payment_intents/search` — Search PaymentIntents

A single PaymentIntent can call the increment authorization endpoint multiple times to further increase the authorized amount. If the incremental authorization succeeds, the PaymentIntent object returns with the updated amount.

---

## 4. Subscriptions (`/v1/subscriptions`)

Creates a new subscription on an existing customer. Each customer can have up to 500 active or scheduled subscriptions.

**Endpoints:**
- `POST /v1/subscriptions` — Create a subscription
- `GET /v1/subscriptions/:id` — Retrieve a subscription
- `POST /v1/subscriptions/:id` — Update a subscription
- `DELETE /v1/subscriptions/:id` — Cancel a subscription
- `GET /v1/subscriptions` — List all subscriptions
- `POST /v1/subscriptions/:id/resume` — Resume a paused subscription
- `GET /v1/subscriptions/search` — Search subscriptions

Canceling a subscription immediately means the customer won't be charged again for the subscription. After it's canceled, you can no longer update the subscription or its metadata.

**Subscription Items** (`/v1/subscription_items`):
- Returns a list of your subscription items for a given subscription.

---

## 5. Invoices (`/v1/invoices`)

The Invoice API provides: Create a preview invoice, Create an invoice, Update an invoice, Retrieve an invoice, List all invoices, Delete a draft invoice, Attach a payment to an Invoice, Finalize an invoice, Mark an invoice as uncollectible, Pay an invoice, Search invoices, Send an invoice for manual payment, and Void an invoice.

**Endpoints:**
- `POST /v1/invoices` — Create an invoice
- `GET /v1/invoices/:id` — Retrieve an invoice
- `POST /v1/invoices/:id` — Update an invoice
- `GET /v1/invoices` — List all invoices
- `DELETE /v1/invoices/:id` — Delete a draft invoice
- `POST /v1/invoices/:id/finalize` — Finalize an invoice
- `POST /v1/invoices/:id/pay` — Pay an invoice
- `POST /v1/invoices/:id/send` — Send an invoice for manual payment
- `POST /v1/invoices/:id/void` — Void an invoice
- `POST /v1/invoices/:id/mark_uncollectible` — Mark as uncollectible
- `POST /v1/invoices/:id/attach_payment` — Attach a payment
- `GET /v1/invoices/search` — Search invoices

The status of the invoice can be one of draft, open, paid, uncollectible, or void.

---

## 6. Products (`/v1/products`)

Subscriptions automatically create Invoices and Payment Intents. They have a Product to model what is being sold, and a Price to determine the interval and amount to charge.

**Endpoints:**
- `POST /v1/products` — Create a product
- `GET /v1/products/:id` — Retrieve a product
- `POST /v1/products/:id` — Update a product
- `DELETE /v1/products/:id` — Delete a product
- `GET /v1/products` — List all products
- `GET /v1/products/search` — Search products

---

## Additional Related Resources

The Stripe API also covers many other resources including: Prices, Coupons, Promotion Codes, Discounts, Tax Codes, Tax Rates, Shipping Rates, Checkout Sessions, Payment Links, Billing Alerts, Credit Notes, Invoice Items, Invoice Line Items, Plans, Quotes, Subscription Schedules, Tax IDs, Test Clocks, Accounts (Connect), and more.

### Key Notes:
- The Payment Intents API is the unifying API for all Stripe products and payment methods. While Stripe isn't deprecating Charges, new features are only available with the Payment Intents API.
- You can use the `expand` parameter on any endpoint that returns expandable fields, including list, create, and update endpoints.

For full details, visit the official [Stripe API Reference](https://docs.stripe.com/api).

Here's a comprehensive overview of the **Instagram Graph API** endpoints, HTTP methods, and required permissions:

---

## Key Endpoints (GET, POST, DELETE)

### Media Endpoints

- **GET** `/{ig-user-id}/media` — List all media (photos, videos, reels) for an account
- **GET** `/{ig-media-id}` — Retrieve a single media object with metadata
- **GET** `/{ig-media-id}/media_product_tags` — Get product tags on media (commerce)
- **POST** `/{ig-user-id}/media` — Create a media container for publishing
- **POST** `/{ig-user-id}/media_publish` — Publish media after the container status is FINISHED


### Comment Endpoints

- **GET** `/{ig-media-id}/comments` — List comments on a media object
- **GET** `/{ig-comment-id}/replies` — Get replies to a specific comment
- **POST** `/{ig-media-id}/comments` — Post a comment to a media object (requires permission)
- **POST** `/{ig-comment-id}/replies` — Reply to a specific comment
- **POST** `/{ig-comment-id}?fields=hidden&hidden=true` — Hide inappropriate comments
- **DELETE** `/{ig-comment-id}` — Delete your own comments


### Other Endpoints
- **GET** `/me` or `/{ig-user-id}` — Retrieve user profile information
- **GET** `/me/media` — Get media for the authenticated user
- **GET** `/{media-id}/insights?metric=impressions,reach,engagement,saves` — Retrieve media performance insights

---

## Required Permission Scopes

**`instagram_basic`** — Lets you read a user's profile information and see their media. This is your starting point for most read-only actions.

**`instagram_content_publish`** — This scope is required to publish photos, videos, or carousels to an Instagram account.

**`instagram_manage_comments`** — Grants the ability to post, hide, unhide, and delete comments on the user's media.

**`instagram_manage_insights`** — Unlocks access to user, post, and story insights. It's essential for any kind of analytics feature.

### How Permissions Map to Actions

Think of scopes as defining which doors your keycard can open. You might have permission to view a user's media (`instagram_basic`), but you can't post comments on their behalf unless they've also granted you the `instagram_manage_comments` scope. This gives users fine-grained control over their own data.

Each scope corresponds to specific API endpoints. You should always make sure the scopes you request line up perfectly with the features your app provides.

---

## Publishing Flow

The content publishing process uses "containers." First create a container, monitor its status, then publish using `POST /{ig-user-id}/media` followed by `POST /{ig-user-id}/media_publish`.

---

## Important Notes

- Requesting only the permissions your app absolutely needs isn't just good practice—it's a requirement for passing Meta's App Review. Asking for too many scopes is one of the most common reasons for rejection.
- The Instagram Graph API lets you publish photos, carousels, and videos directly to Instagram Business and Creator accounts. What you can't do is post Stories or Reels—that functionality is still off-limits via the API.
- Permissions and features are used to manage endpoint authorization. Before your app may utilize an endpoint to access an app user's Instagram data, you must first get all of the required permissions. The app user must then allow your app those rights, after which you can query the endpoints to access the user's data.

For full official documentation, refer to:
- [Meta Instagram Graph API Reference](https://developers.facebook.com/docs/instagram-platform/instagram-graph-api/reference/ig-comment/)
- [Postman Instagram API Collection](https://www.postman.com/meta/instagram/documentation/6yqw8pt/instagram-api)

Here's a summary of what I found about the **QuickBooks Online API v3** endpoints, entity operations, and key entities like Customer, Invoice, Bill, Payment, and Vendor:

## QuickBooks Online API v3 — Overview

The QuickBooks Online Accounting API uses the REST framework to provide apps with access to the customer-facing features in the software. The API is broken down into several entities that you can use to access different parts of the app.

It utilizes standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and returns JSON objects, making it compatible with a wide range of languages and frameworks, like Python, Java, TypeScript, and PHP.

### Key Entities & Endpoints

It consists of endpoints that let you access and perform actions on a variety of resources within QuickBooks—from bills to invoices to journal entries to vendor credits.

It has many different endpoints for each feature, such as invoices, accounts, bills, customers, and employees; and each endpoint has different requirements.

#### Example Endpoint Patterns:
Key endpoints include: Read a customer: `GET /v3/company/<realmID>/customer/<customerId>`, Create an invoice: `POST /v3/company/<realmID>/invoice`, and Query a bill: `GET /v3/company/<realmID>/query?query=<selectStatement>`.

### Supported Operations

The API provides unique abilities apart from basic create, read, update, and delete (CRUD) operations, such as single requests (where the server individually processes and responds to each single request).

It also supports query requests, which use the Intuit SQL-like query language to send a query to the server.

### Key Entity Details

**Customer:**
Customers are the consumers of services or products offered by businesses. QuickBooks includes parent and sub-customer entities for simple and detailed classification. Key attributes include DisplayName, GivenName, PrimaryEmailAddr, etc.

**Invoice:**
An invoice represents a sales form where customers pay for a product or service. QuickBooks applies specific business rules to this entity. Key attributes include DocNumber, BillEmail, TrackingNum, etc.

**Bill:**
The Bill entity has various details and fields about individual bills, such as vendor reference, currency reference, transaction date, amount, and due date. Attributes include VendorRef, TotalAmt, Balance, and more.

**Payment:**
It records the payment for customers against single or multiple invoices and credit memos in QuickBooks. It can be a full update or a sparse update. Attributes include TotalAmt, PaymentMethodRef, Unapplied Amt, and more.

**Vendor:**
A vendor is a seller from whom the company purchases any service or product. QuickBooks applies certain business rules to this entity. Key attributes include DisplayName, GivenName, PrimaryEmailAddr, etc.

### Update Operations (Full vs. Sparse)

QuickBooks Online provides two updates: full update and sparse update. The sparse update operation provides the ability to update a subset of attributes for a given object; only those specified in the request are updated. Missing attributes are left untouched. This is in contrast to the full update operation, where elements missing from the request are cleared.

### Batch & Bulk Operations

You can batch operations such as creating an Invoice, updating a Customer, etc. The maximum batch size is 25 objects.

### Pagination & Queries

The maximum number of entities that can be returned in a response is 1000. If the result size is not specified, the default number is 100. If a query returns many entities, fetch the entities in chunks using pagination.

### Official API Reference Links

The official Intuit developer documentation for individual entity endpoints can be found at:
- **Bill**: https://developer.intuit.com/app/developer/qbo/docs/api/accounting/all-entities/bill
- **Invoice**: https://developer.intuit.com/app/developer/qbo/docs/api/accounting/all-entities/invoice
- **Payment**: https://developer.intuit.com/app/developer/qbo/docs/api/accounting/all-entities/payment
- **Overview**: https://developer.intuit.com/app/developer/qbo/docs/learn/explore-the-quickbooks-online-api

### Webhooks

Webhooks act as instant messengers, delivering information in real-time straight to your app whenever an event occurs in your end user's QuickBooks account. You can use this data to perform further actions or logic. For example, if your user creates a new invoice, vendor, or customer, you can instantly get a notification, which can be used for actions such as automatically updating your accounting system, sending a confirmation email, or triggering a workflow.

Here is a comprehensive overview of the key LinkedIn API v2 endpoints and the OAuth 2.0 scopes required for each:

---

## LinkedIn API v2 Endpoints & Required Scopes

### 1. `/v2/me` (Profile Endpoint — Legacy)

The legacy approach used the `r_liteprofile` and `r_emailaddress` scopes and then called `https://api.linkedin.com/v2/me`. However, LinkedIn has switched its sign-in flow to use OpenID Connect (OIDC). The new supported scopes are `openid`, `profile`, and `email`, and the new endpoint to fetch user profile is `https://api.linkedin.com/v2/userinfo`.

- **Legacy scopes:** `r_liteprofile`, `r_emailaddress`
- **New (OIDC) scopes:** `openid`, `profile`, `email`
- The old scopes and endpoints no longer work for new apps.

**Usage example:**
Once you've obtained an access token, you can make authenticated API requests by including an Authorization header: `curl -X GET 'https://api.linkedin.com/v2/me' -H 'Authorization: Bearer {INSERT_TOKEN}'`

---

### 2. `/v2/userinfo` (OpenID Connect Profile Endpoint — Current)

All new apps must use OpenID Connect scopes: `openid` (required for authentication), `profile` (basic profile info, replaces `r_liteprofile`), `email` (replaces `r_emailaddress`), and profile information for authenticated members is now retrieved via the `/v2/userinfo` endpoint.

- **Required scopes:** `openid`, `profile`, `email`

---

### 3. `/v2/ugcPosts` (User Generated Content / Posts)

For all shares created on LinkedIn, the request will always be a POST request to the User Generated Content (UGC) API: `POST https://api.linkedin.com/v2/ugcPosts`.

**Required scopes:**

- **For personal posting:** Make sure to request the `w_member_social` scope. You can add it through the Developer Portal by navigating to the Products tab and adding the "Share on LinkedIn" product, which grants `w_member_social`.

- **For organization posting:** The `w_organization_social` scope is needed to post, comment, and like posts on behalf of an organization. This is restricted to organizations in which the authenticated member has one of the following company page roles: ADMINISTRATOR, DIRECT_SPONSORED_CONTENT_POSTER, RECRUITING_POSTER.

- **For reading posts:** `r_member_social` is used to retrieve posts, comments, and likes on behalf of an authenticated member (this permission is granted to select developers only). `r_organization_social` is used to retrieve organizations' posts, comments, and likes.

**Note:** The Posts API replaces the ugcPosts API. See the Migration Guide for more details.

**Headers required:**
All API requests are represented in protocol 2.0.0 and require the header `X-Restli-Protocol-Version: 2.0.0`.

---

### 4. `/v2/shares` (Shares API — Legacy)

The `/v2/shares` endpoint was used for creating and retrieving shares. The API endpoint is `GET https://api.linkedin.com/v2/shares?q=owners&owners=urn:li:company:{id}`.

**Required scopes:**

- **For member shares:** To post share on own profile you need `w_member_social` permission.
- **For organization shares:** `w_organization_social` permission is required, and the user should have one of the following company page roles: ADMINISTRATOR, DIRECT_SPONSORED_CONTENT_POSTER, RECRUITING_POSTER.
- **For reading organization shares:** The app must have access to the Marketing Developer Platform product and its scopes (typically `r_organization_social`).

---

### 5. `/v2/organizationalEntityFollowerStatistics` (Follower Statistics)

URL: `https://api.linkedin.com/v2/organizationalEntityFollowerStatistics?q=organizationalEntity&organizationalEntity={OrganizationURN}`

**Required scopes:**

- You need to add the `r_organization_social` scope to your access token to access this endpoint.
- The scope `rw_organization_admin` is required to access the `organizationalEntityFollowerStatistics` endpoint to retrieve lifetime follower information.
- Using scopes `r_organizational_social` and `rw_organizational_admin`, the `organizationalEntityFollowerStatistics` and `organizationPageStatistics` endpoints work successfully.

---

## Summary Table

| Endpoint | Required Scopes | Notes |
|----------|----------------|-------|
| `/v2/me` | `r_liteprofile` (legacy) | Deprecated for new apps |
| `/v2/userinfo` | `openid`, `profile`, `email` | Current replacement for `/v2/me` |
| `/v2/ugcPosts` (create) | `w_member_social` (personal) / `w_organization_social` (org) | Legacy; being replaced by Posts API |
| `/v2/ugcPosts` (read) | `r_member_social` / `r_organization_social` | Select developers only for member |
| `/v2/shares` (create) | `w_member_social` / `w_organization_social` | Legacy |
| `/v2/shares` (read) | `r_organization_social` | Via Marketing Developer Platform |
| `/v2/organizationalEntityFollowerStatistics` | `r_organization_social`, `rw_organization_admin` | Requires company page admin role |

---

## Important Updates

- LinkedIn now publishes new Marketing API versions monthly using a YYYYMM format, and each version is supported for at least one year.
- Legacy unversioned Marketing APIs (base path `https://api.linkedin.com/v2/`) have been fully sunset.
- All APIs require the request header `Linkedin-Version: {Version in YYYYMM format}` and `X-Restli-Protocol-Version: 2.0.0`.
- The "Sign in with LinkedIn" product gives access to `r_liteprofile` and `r_emailaddress` scopes (legacy). The "Share on LinkedIn" product gives access to the `w_member_social` scope. Remaining scopes are obtained from the Marketing Developer Platform product.
